agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days, down from 7 years. Framed as infrastructure optimization but intersects with legal retention obligations, contractual commitments to B2B customers, and consumer rights under EU/UK regulation. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on this data for their own compliance reporting.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Lifecycle Management — spans Order Fulfillment, Compliance & Audit, Customer Service, and Partner Data Services bounded contexts.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact documenting a delivery transaction: proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient confirmation, chain of custody, item manifest, and associated metadata."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient at the correct location. Legally significant document in disputes, chargebacks, and insurance claims."},{"term":"Data Retention Period","definition":"The mandatory or contractual minimum duration that records must be preserved before eligible for deletion."},{"term":"Statutory Retention Obligation","definition":"A legally mandated minimum period for which specific record types must be kept, violation of which constitutes a compliance breach."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by business customers to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations — making our retention policy a dependency in their compliance chain."},{"term":"Limitation Period","definition":"The legal window during which a claim (contractual, tortious, or statutory) can be brought. Records needed to defend or pursue such claims must be retained through this period."},{"term":"Chain of Custody","definition":"The documented trail of who handled a parcel at each stage, critical for loss/damage claims and insurance."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records in EU/UK jurisdictions must be retained for the duration required by applicable statutory retention obligations, which range from 5 to 10 years depending on the record type and jurisdiction.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal constraint, not a business policy. EU tax law (Directive 2006/112/EC) requires VAT-relevant transaction records to be kept for 5-10 years depending on member state. UK HMRC requires 6 years. Violating this corrupts the organization's legal standing.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record subject to statutory retention shall be deleted before the applicable statutory retention period has expired."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Proof of Delivery records must be retained for at least the full limitation period during which a legal claim related to that delivery could be brought.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Under the UK Limitation Act 1980, contract claims have a 6-year limitation period. EU member states have similar periods (3-10 years). Deleting POD before the limitation period expires destroys evidence needed to defend against claims, creating direct legal and financial liability.","testable_assertion":"No POD record shall be deleted while any applicable limitation period for claims related to that delivery remains open."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customer delivery data must be retained for the duration specified in the data processing agreement or service contract with that customer.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Contractual obligations to B2B customers are legally binding. If B2B customers rely on our data for their own compliance reporting (e.g., 7-year tax audit cycles), deleting their data constitutes a breach of contract and potentially causes them regulatory violations.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record associated with a B2B customer shall be deleted before the contractual retention commitment to that customer has been satisfied."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"GDPR and UK GDPR require a lawful basis for both retention AND deletion — data cannot be deleted if a legal obligation requires its retention, and cannot be retained beyond necessity without lawful basis.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Data protection law creates a two-way constraint: you must delete when there's no lawful basis to keep, but you must NOT delete when a legal obligation mandates retention. A blanket 90-day deletion policy violates the retention side of this obligation.","testable_assertion":"Every deletion decision must evaluate and document the applicable lawful basis; records with an active legal retention obligation must be excluded from auto-deletion."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Delivery records involved in active disputes, claims, investigations, or litigation holds must never be auto-deleted regardless of any retention policy.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying evidence subject to a litigation hold or active dispute is spoliation of evidence, which can result in adverse inference, sanctions, and criminal liability.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record flagged with an active dispute, claim, investigation, or litigation hold shall be eligible for auto-deletion."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A consumer files a damage claim 89 days after delivery; the record is auto-deleted on day 90 before the claim is fully resolved.","expected_behavior":"Records involved in any open claim must be excluded from auto-deletion. The deletion policy must check for active disputes before purging."},{"scenario":"A B2B customer is audited by their national tax authority 4 years after deliveries occurred and requests historical delivery data for compliance evidence.","expected_behavior":"Data must still be available. B2B contractual retention commitments override any internal storage optimization policy."},{"scenario":"HMRC or an EU tax authority requests delivery records for a VAT audit covering the previous 6 years.","expected_behavior":"All records must be producible. Failure to produce constitutes a compliance violation with potential penalties."},{"scenario":"Cross-border delivery where origin country requires 5-year retention but destination country requires 10-year retention.","expected_behavior":"The longest applicable retention period governs. The record must be retained for 10 years."},{"scenario":"A GDPR data subject access request (DSAR) is filed for delivery records that were auto-deleted at 90 days but should have been retained under a legal obligation.","expected_behavior":"This creates a dual violation: failure to satisfy the DSAR AND failure to meet the legal retention obligation. Both are reportable to the ICO/DPA."},{"scenario":"Insurance claim for lost/damaged high-value shipment filed 18 months after delivery.","expected_behavior":"Chain of custody records and POD must be available to support or defend the insurance claim. 90-day deletion would have destroyed this evidence."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. (1) Regulatory fines: GDPR fines up to 4% of global annual turnover for systematic non-compliance with retention obligations. (2) Tax penalties: inability to produce records for VAT audits across EU/UK jurisdictions. (3) Litigation exposure: inability to defend against delivery dispute claims within 6-year limitation periods — estimated exposure scales with delivery volume. (4) B2B contract breach: potential damages claims from business customers whose own compliance is broken by our premature deletion. (5) Insurance claim denials: inability to substantiate claims for lost/damaged goods.","operational":"HIGH. (1) Storage cost savings are real but modest compared to regulatory and legal risk. (2) A compliant tiered retention policy (hot/warm/cold storage with lifecycle rules) achieves 70-90% of the cost savings without compliance risk. (3) Implementing a proper retention policy requires classifying records by jurisdiction, customer type, and legal obligation — this is NOT a simple infrastructure change.","user":"CRITICAL for B2B customers who depend on delivery data availability for their own compliance reporting. Contract violations could trigger customer churn of highest-value accounts. B2C consumers lose ability to reference historical deliveries for disputes, returns, or warranty claims.","risk":"EXTREME. This change as proposed would create systematic legal non-compliance across every EU and UK jurisdiction the platform operates in. It is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's legal compliance posture. The framing as 'no business logic changes' is incorrect and dangerous.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Loss of delivery data they contractually depend on for their own compliance reporting. Breach of data processing agreements. Could trigger regulatory violations for THEM, leading to contract termination, damages claims, and reputational harm."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Loss of historical delivery records needed for disputes, warranty claims, and chargebacks. Degraded customer service experience."},{"group":"Legal & Compliance Team","impact":"Organization exposed to regulatory enforcement across multiple jurisdictions simultaneously. Must be consulted before any retention policy change."},{"group":"Finance & Tax Team","impact":"Inability to produce delivery records for VAT audits. Direct financial penalty exposure."},{"group":"Customer Service","impact":"Unable to look up historical deliveries to resolve customer inquiries, complaints, and claims."},{"group":"Insurance & Claims","impact":"Unable to substantiate or defend delivery-related insurance claims beyond 90 days."},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"The proposed simple deletion job must be replaced with a jurisdiction-aware, contract-aware, dispute-aware tiered retention system — significantly more complex than described."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the proposal as stated contains a fundamental contradiction: it claims 'no business logic changes' while proposing a change that directly violates multiple statutory retention obligations, contractual commitments, and data protection requirements. The spec is not just incomplete — it is actively wrong about the nature of the change. Cannot produce acceptance criteria for a proposal that would create systematic legal non-compliance.","high_confidence_areas":["EU/UK statutory retention obligations exceed 90 days — this is settled law","B2B customers using data for compliance reporting creates contractual retention dependencies","GDPR creates dual obligations around both retention and deletion","Limitation periods for contract claims in UK/EU exceed 90 days"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention periods per member state (vary from 5-10 years for tax records)","Specific contractual terms in B2B agreements (not provided)","Whether any current records are already subject to litigation holds","Current storage cost breakdown and potential savings from tiered approach"],"assumptions":["The platform processes VAT-relevant transactions","B2B contracts include data retention commitments or SLAs around data availability","No legal review has been conducted on this proposal","The 7-year current policy was originally set with legal input"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal is framed as 'infrastructure optimization — no business logic changes' but a 90-day auto-deletion policy in EU/UK markets directly contradicts statutory retention obligations (5-10 years for tax records), contractual obligations to B2B customers, and limitation periods for legal claims (6 years UK). This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a compliance-critical business rule change that has been misclassified.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","B2C Consumers","Legal & Compliance","Finance & Tax","Customer Service","Insurance & Claims"],"options":["A: Reject the 90-day blanket deletion and maintain 7-year retention as-is","B: Implement a tiered retention policy — hot storage (0-90 days), warm storage (90 days-2 years), cold/archive storage (2-7+ years) — achieving cost savings while maintaining compliance","C: Engage Legal to define minimum retention periods per jurisdiction, record type, and customer contract, then build a rules-based retention engine"],"recommendation":"Option C is the correct long-term approach, with Option B as an interim measure. Option A is acceptable if cost pressure is not urgent. The 90-day blanket deletion (the current proposal) must be REJECTED — it would create systematic legal non-compliance across all operating jurisdictions. The engineering team should partner with Legal and Compliance to define retention rules before any deletion logic is implemented."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Proposal would auto-delete delivery records required by EU/UK tax law to be retained 5-10 years","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: Statutory retention obligations"},{"violation":"Proposal would destroy Proof of Delivery within 90 days while legal claims can be brought for up to 6 years (UK) or longer (EU)","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Limitation period retention"},{"violation":"Proposal would delete B2B customer data that customers depend on for their own compliance reporting, breaching contractual obligations","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: B2B contractual retention commitments"},{"violation":"Blanket 90-day deletion ignores GDPR requirement to retain data when a legal obligation mandates it","business_rule_broken":"BR-4: GDPR dual retention/deletion obligation"},{"violation":"No mechanism described to exempt records under active disputes or litigation holds from auto-deletion","business_rule_broken":"BR-5: Litigation hold and dispute exemption"}]}
